title: Welcome To My Blog
text:
Welcome!
Another blog from a person interested in science. What is different here? Well, let’s see. I do not know yet myself. Maybe nothing except for the fact that written blogs run out of fashion anyways and my expectation is that nobody’s going to read it anyways. This reduces the pressure immensely. There is the old joke that one should store some money in ones thesis at the library. It’s a save place!

title: Welcome To My Blog
text:
What can I exact?
I will write mostly about exercise and human movement sciences related to health. I also have a strong interest in psychology and statistics but that doesn’t mean I am an expert in any of those areas.
Some posts will be of practical importance and deal with physical exercise or athletic training. Others might be more theoretical or philosophical and might not give you the definitive answer you are looking for. In any case, I try to back up my ideas with scientific theories or direct findings. I will make sure to cite the origin of the knowledge-base I tap into as good as possible (until no original thought can be thought anymore).

title: Welcome To My Blog
section: Why do you write this blog?
text:
Why do you write this blog?
Pure scholarship. Allan Watts talks about the idea of studying as leisure and how scholarship contradicts itself without the playfulness invited (Allan Watts). It’s hard to grasp for me but I try to follow the principle here. The blog is a way to try ideas out. Let them sink. It’s low risk and low cost compared to writing a book or a paper but I still might get some feedback from outside. That might be the main idea (as far as I understand myself).
So, have fun reading!

title: Post With Code
text:
This is a post with executable code.

1 + 1

[1] 2

title: About
text:
Dear reader,
this blog is my effort to make sense of everything exercise science, human movement and psychology. There is no direct purpose behind this blog. It is said that many great scientist answered in a similar way when asked about the practical implications of their findings:
We don’t do it because it is important - We do it because it is interesting.
This blog is mainly a personal experiment. (No, I do not need a hypothesis for that.) Feel free to leave comments or send me a message. No likes or subscriptions are needed. Only Buy Me A Coffee when we meet in person.



ReuseCC BY 4.0

title: Welcome to the Moving Science Blog
text:
What’s special about that blog? Honestly, I do not know yet! Maybe nothing, except that written blogs run out of fashion. I expect nobody to read it. This reduces the pressure immensely! The old joke is that if you are looking for a safe place to store money, put it in your thesis at the library. It will be there in times of need.
I hope to store some thoughts here and discover what happens to them when they are exposed to the virtual world. Maybe an AI system will pick them up, and my mind will reincarnate in silicon form a couple of decades from now. Maybe some posts reach other biological minds, leading to interesting conversations and discussions. Maybe this blog stays there for a couple of months and vanishes in the vastness of cyberspace. I do not feel confident about any of these predictions. But I am sure my future self will find a nice story about that blog and why it turned out the way it did…

Some posts will be of practical importance and deal with physical exercise or athletic training. Others might be more theoretical or philosophical and might not give you the definitive answer you are looking for. In any case, I try to back up my ideas with scientific theories or direct findings. I will make sure to cite the origin of the knowledge-base I tap into as good as possible (until no original thought can be thought anymore).

title: Welcome To My Blog
section: Why do you write this blog?
text:
Why do you write this blog?
Pure scholarship! Allan Watts talked about studying as leisure and how scholarship contradicts itself when playfulness is not invited (Allan Watts). It’s hard to grasp, but I am trying to follow this principle. The blog is a way to try out ideas - let them sink. It’s low risk and cost compared to writing a book or a paper. But I still might get some feedback from outside. That might be the main idea (as far as I understand).
So, I wish myself fun, and I am happy to share it :-)

title: On Scientific Writing and Blogs
text:
The first scientific journal was installed by the Royal Society in 1665, and today, scientific publications in peer-reviewed journals are the most important currency in science. I guess it is with good reason. Publishers made it possible for the community to distribute the knowledge (rather slowly, one would argue). It is conceivable that our research progress around the world would have been way slower without those printed journals.
Today the big publishers get a lot of bad press. The scientific community seems to be especially frustrated with the current situation. Not a week goes in which I do not hear a colleague complaining or making self-aware jokes about the publishing system and its effects on academia. But in this post, I do not want to go into that in more detail. There is a lot of discussion about it, and everybody seems to know what Elsevier, Springer, and Whiley should do to make the world a better place. It is a bit like during the soccer world-cup during which everyone becomes a sports analyst and coach.
Here I want to approach the problem from another perspective. What can scientists do to improve the situation?
Maybe not much, because your publication list is what makes you a successful scientist. Publish or perish! First of all, the truth of this motto is jet to be determined. As the next generation of scientists climbs the academic ladder, they increasingly understand that journal publications are just one of the things that make good researchers. At one point, political decision-makers and funding agencies will understand too. But the first steps have to be done from the ground up.

title: On Scientific Writing and Blogs
text:
The first scientific journal was installed by the Royal Society in 1665, and today, scientific publications in peer-reviewed journals are the most important currency in science. I guess it is with good reason. Publishers made it possible for the community to distribute the knowledge (rather slowly, one would argue). It is conceivable that our research progress around the world would have been way slower without those printed journals.
Today the big publishers get a lot of bad press. The scientific community seems to be especially frustrated with the current situation. Not a week goes in which I do not hear a colleague complaining or making self-aware jokes about the publishing system and its effects on academia. But in this post, I do not want to go into that in more detail. There is a lot of discussion about it, and everybody seems to know what Elsevier, Springer, and Whiley should do to make the world a better place. It is a bit like during the soccer world-cup during which everyone becomes a sports analyst and coach.
Here I want to approach the problem from another perspective. What can scientists do to improve the situation?
Maybe not much, because your publication list is what makes you a successful scientist. Publish or perish! First of all, the truth of this motto is jet to be determined. As the next generation of scientists climbs the academic ladder, they increasingly understand that journal publications are just one of the things that make good researchers. At one point, political decision-makers and funding agencies will understand too. But the first steps have to be done from the ground up.

title: On Scientific Writing and Blogs
section: Double Committement - Life Outside the Ivory Tower
text:
Double Committement - Life Outside the Ivory Tower
As I have written on the front page, science, and scholarship are activities of leisure. But scientists have to live on something too. Traditionally, many Western scientists have lived from family wealth. Many did the experiments and observations out of curiosity, leisure or to reach some kind of fame. Nowadays things are different, obviously. While academic researchers do enjoy prestige in some regards, the money that can be made is relatively limited. This is especially true concerning extensive education, high working hours, and the low probability of reaching the top of the food chain, which mostly means getting tenure. An alternative route for many is going into industry. This is a more realistic route for some research areas than others and nearly impossible for some.



academia vs industry


Sometimes, there is some middle ground. Some scientists hit the “jackpot” and to both. They enjoy the freedom of academia and, as a related side-show, they work in the private sector. For health and performance-related fields (the ones that I am most interested in) like sport- and exercise science, psychology, and nutrition, this might mean giving seminars, selling books, working with supplement companies, or leveraging one’s research idea into profitable products.
I can tell you that this is huge in this sector, and probably some big names come to your mind that do exactly that. So, in essence, they are selling the expertise they have carved out over years or decades of work. Fair thing, right? Let me make this clear: Some of independent scientists directly use the leverage they gain to fund their research with good intentions. Some claim that their breakthroughs would have not been possible in the traditional way because nobody beliefed in their work and they couldn’t get funding.
But researchers are faulty human beings. No matter if they are funded by a national funding agency, by their personal company, or by personal beliefs and the urge to be right. We are invested in our ideas and hypothesis. I do not know if everybody iss, but I certainly am1. I do not believe that being invested in an idea is completely avoidable. But caution is advisable if scientists sell products (or knowledge) related to their research without doing that directly as or in their role of the research institution. It might sound like a good thing if popular Stanford professors state that their podcast is independent of their role as a researcher, but I am not convinced that can be true. Not only because they (probably) have no chance but being the same person!
Sure, “research is me-search” - and personal investment might come with higher motivation, drive and creativity. This makes the objective work vulnerable and contaminates your role as a researcher. Another profession knows about that for hundreds and thousands of years. So, in many traditions, priests are not allowed to have a family or sex. That’s how they stay independent and hard to bribe (in theory).

title: On Scientific Writing and Blogs
section: But Why is Selling Books Bad?
text:
But Why is Selling Books Bad?
Making research available to the public is a significant component of modern science and is incentivized and with good reason. We want educated and responsible citizens!
BUT WHY SELL IT??? That’s the whole point.
We, want people - all people - to get access to the knowledge base. We are unhappy with the power of big publishing houses. But, it is hard to change that in the case of peer-reviewed articles without risking one’s career. We talk about the FAIR principles within the ivory tower of science. But why not expand these principles beyond that?
We can and should leverage all those problems into unrestricted publications via blogs or free internet books.
Publish your teaching openly: I myself started putting one of my undergraduate seminars into writing and publishing it on Bookdown2. This makes my seminar not only more transparent for students but also allows the information to be updated on a regular basis. Even after finishing the course, students can easily access the information. One could argue that you no longer need to go to the seminar as all the information is accessible for free on the Internet. But in my opinion, that is the case anyway. I can not tell our students any exercise training secrets anyway. Nobody should go to university for that explicit, teachable information anymore. We have passed that point a long time ago! The good news is that we can focus on experimental learning and carve out competencies3!
Writing blog posts about your research: Traditional scientific journal articles can only withstand so much information. The general consensus in many fields is not to spoil the reader with too much personal experience, different challenges during different phases of the project. I think even in pre-prints and other publishing formats that are more researcher centered, we hold on to the traditional writing style like using passive voice as if everything was executed by a emotionless and error-free research-robot. In reality there is often some kind of thinking, tweaking, rethinking and frustration involved. Sharing those things might improve project science. Writing those protocols in a personal way might not only help yourself resolving some of the project related issues but also make the work of the grad-student working on a similar project at another university easier. Humanizing our daily work by writing about it, is also a way to make science and scientists more relate-able for people outside of academia. We do have this problem of trust towards science and just informing people “how reality works” does not do the trick to improve that, in my opinion. But being relate-able is also something we should work for within Team Science. Seeing that all science (not only my project) is messy. There are struggles that go beyond low statistical power and deadlines. I personally would love to see more unrestricted, writing from scientists. I would love to see less profit oriented side-gigs and more real content - raw, personal and messy as it is and I hope you do too. Because that is what I will be trying with this blog.

title: On Scientific Writing and Blogs
text:
Footnotes


and if there is one thing I learned from stats, it’s that, most likely, I am a typical person and others think and act similarly.↩︎
It is in German and addresses bachelor sports and human movement science students. It is work growing and work in progress. But you can find it here if you are interested in exercise related Personal Coaching.↩︎

title: Welcome to the Moving Science Blog
text:
What can you expect?
I will mostly write about the science of exercise and human movement related to health. I also have strong interests in psychology and statistics, but that doesn’t mean I am an expert in any of them.
Some posts will be of practical nature and deal with physical exercise, rehabilitation or athletic training. Others might be more theoretical or philosophical at times. In any case, I will try to back up my ideas with scientific theories and my readings of the current literature. I will do as I was taught and cite the origin of thoughts as well as possible (until no original thought can be thought anymore).

title: Welcome to the Moving Science Blog
section: Why do I write this blog?
text:
Why do I write this blog?
Pure scholarship! Allan Watts was talking about studying as leisure and how scholarship contradicts itself when playfulness is not invited (Watts 1995). It’s hard to grasp, but I am trying to follow this principle. The blog is a way to try out ideas - let them express themselves and then rest and sink1. It seems like low risk and low cost compared to writing a book or a paper. Not writing (or not making it public) would rob me of the whole experience. The intention to publish it makes one think harder and care about the writing.
So, I wish myself fun, and I am happy to share it

title: Outside Academia
text:
As I have written on the front page, science and scholarship are activities of leisure. However, scientists must also live on something. Many of the known scientists of the past lived from family wealth. They did the experiments and observations out of curiosity, leisure, fame, glory, or to serve god. Nowadays, things are different. While academic researchers do enjoy prestige in some respects, the financial rewards are relatively limited. This is especially true concerning extensive education, high working hours, and the low probability of reaching the top of the food chain, which mostly means securing tenure. For many, an alternative route is entering the industry. This is a more realistic route for some research areas than others and nearly impossible for some.



academia vs industry


Sometimes, there is some middle ground. Some scientists hit the “jackpot” and do both. They enjoy the freedom of academia and, as a related sideshow, they work in the private sector. For health and performance-related fields (the ones that I am most interested in) like sports and exercise science, psychology, and nutrition, this might involve giving seminars, selling books, working with supplement companies, or leveraging one’s research ideas into other profitable products.
This is more common than one might think. Probably, some big names come to your mind that do exactly that. So, in essence, they are selling the expertise they have carved out over years or decades of work. Fair thing, right? Let me make this clear: Some independent scientists directly use the leverage they gain to fund their research with good intentions. Some claim that their breakthroughs would not have been possible in the traditional way because nobody believed in their work and they couldn’t get funding.
Researchers are faulty human beings. Regardless of whether they are funded by a national funding agency or their own company - driven by personal beliefs and the urge to be right. We are invested in our ideas and hypotheses. I do not know if everybody is, but I certainly am1. I do not believe that being invested in an idea is completely avoidable. But caution is advisable if scientists sell products (or knowledge) related to their research. It might sound like a good thing if popular Stanford professors claim that their podcast is independent of their role as researchers, but I am not convinced that can be true. Not only because they (probably) have no chance, but being the same person!
Sure, “research is me-search” and personal investment might come with higher motivation, drive and creativity. This makes the objective work vulnerable and contaminates your role as a researcher. Another profession has known about that for hundreds of years. That might be why, in many traditions, priests are not allowed to have a family or sex. That’s how they stay independent and hard to bribe (in theory).


Making research available to the public is a significant component of modern science and is incentivized and with good reason. We want educated and responsible citizens!
BUT WHY SELL IT??? That’s the whole point.
We want people - all people - to get access to the knowledge base. We are dissatisfied with the influence of large publishing houses. However, it is hard to change that in the case of peer-reviewed articles without risking one’s career. We talk about the FAIR principles within the ivory tower of science. But why not expand these principles beyond that?
We can and should leverage all those problems into unrestricted publications, such as blogs or free online books.
Publish your teaching openly: I myself started putting one of my undergraduate seminars into written form and publishing it on Bookdown2. This makes my seminar not only more transparent for students but also allows the information to be updated on a regular basis. Even after finishing the course, students can easily access the information. One could argue that you no longer need to attend the seminar, as all the information is accessible for free on the internet. But in my opinion, that is the case anyway. I can not tell our students any exercise training secrets anyway. Nobody should go to university for that explicit, teachable information anymore. We passed that point a long time ago! The good news is that we can focus on experimental learning and carve out competencies3!
Writing blog posts about your research: Traditional scientific journal articles can only withstand so much information. The general consensus in many fields is not to spoil the reader with too much personal experience, different challenges during various phases of the project. I think even in pre-prints and other publishing formats that are more researcher-centered, we hold on to the traditional writing style, like using passive voice as if everything was executed by an emotionless and error-free research robot. In reality, there is often some kind of thinking, tweaking, rethinking, and frustration involved. Sharing those things might improve project science. Writing those protocols in a personal way might not only help you resolve some of the project-related issues but also make the work of the grad student working on a similar project at another university easier. Humanizing our daily work by writing about it is also a way to make science and scientists more relatable for people outside of academia. We have a huge problem of trust in science. Simply informing people on “how reality works” does not do the trick to improve that. Playing with open cards might!
But being relatable is also something we should work for within Team Science. Seeing that all science (not only my project) is messy. There are struggles that go beyond low statistical power and deadlines. I personally would love to see more unrestricted writing from scientists. I would love to see less profit-oriented side gigs and more real content - raw, personal, and messy

title: A discussion of Training
text: Most of my extensive (formal and informal) education falls into the realm of training science. So I want to start right there and give you a introduction to what I mean with training.

title: A discussion of Training
text:
Three sites of adaptations:
Within the model of training, I would like to list three sites of adaptation: Biological capacities, function, and structures. An example of capacity would be the ability to run a marathon or go to the toilet without help. But it could also be the ability to read or write faster or hold one’s attention on the breath. With biological function, I very broadly refer to the ability to change internal states that can be measured1. A typical example could be the control of heart rate or the blood glucose levels during different demanding situations. Lastly, biological structure ended up with a separate category. Here, I refer to preferable changes in tissue size, number of sub-units or architecture like increased muscle volume or number of red blood cells. The interesting thing about how training is typically defined is that it has to be goal-oriented. There is a motivation and a reason to structure one’s actions to reach the defined sites of adaptations. Following this textbook-like definition, someone MUST have a conscious, explicit idea of what to work on and how to achieve this! So, there is no such thing as training by chance. Also, adaptions to physical activity that are not led by a systematic approach to reach a goal can not be considered training.

title: A discussion of Training
section: Training vs other forms of change
text:
Training vs other forms of change
We can now compare training to a very similar process. Learning can be unconscious. It can happen out of internal or external demands to the system/organism, not just out of long-term planning. Changes occurring by learning should be differentiated from developmental or fatigue-induced adaptations. Obviously, learning can also be pre-contemplated and goal-oriented, just like training. The difference might be that learning involves long-term changes in behaviors or expressing completely new behaviors. Training is sometimes thought of as a process that can only be done after a pattern already emerges, i.e., was already learned. But this is not a strict rule, and maybe we could define training as a subcategory of learning.
The funny thing about the difference between training and learning is that the predetermined goal that is inherent in training does not have to be conscious to the organism performing the training. A rat or a pigeon could also be trained without it understanding the training process. Actually, before the focus on physiological adaptation was popular, training principles were also heavily influenced by psychology. This was during a time when behaviorism was the proper way to frame human learning2 (Krüger 2006). Training principles for a human (or a rat, as the most influential scientists of that time didn’t see much of a difference) were radically different from today’s viewpoint. A person could be trained by different forms of conditioning without the person being aware of it. The scientist or coach could play god in this scenario and transform the person into “whatever they like”3. Still, somebody has to be aware of the training process. A religious person believing in a greater plan that unfolds might call any learning process also training. But for the rest of us, there is a difference.

title: A discussion of Training
text:
Measure and manage
This all leads us back to the problem of tracking expected and unexpected changes in capacity, function, and structure. Typically, we only measure the thing we previously specified as important. Surely, there are more general ways to measure ANY changes in the categories previously defined. But this data-mining approach to tracking, which often comes up naturally in practitioners, can actually not fall under the umbrella of training-induced changes. One could even argue that cross-training effects are a complete misnomer. If you train to get stronger in lifting weights and after a couple of weeks you realize you have become a better runner, this can not be a training effect by most textbooks. The definition of training seems to be very much in line with a deductive, hypothesis-driven way of doing science. Clearly, we do not handle it like that and mix it up with inductive methods of observing post-hoc what happened, to reason the effects of the training process. I think this is actually a good thing. Certainly, historically, we mostly understood training principles from an inductive perspective. We built our ideas on how to train by observing changes time and time again.

title: A discussion of Training
text:
Footnotes


This does not mean that we have to measure it during the training process. But it sould be measurable in principle. For example we could measure the transport of oxygen to a target tissue in the lab. But we will not do that for every intervention we plan for every person undergoing the training intervention.↩︎
Especially in the USA. In Europe Gestalt Psychology and other phenomenological approaches were more prominent, as far as I understand.↩︎
Watson famously claimed to be able to make anything out of a healthy infant↩︎

title: Exercise Program by AI
text:
At the beginning of each semester, I ask students what they expect learning in the seminar Training Processes that I teach at the University of Vienna. The answer I hear most often is that they want to be able to write a good exercise program. At that point in their education, they have already learned the foundations of exercise physiology, biomechanics, and training science. They know the basic training principles, prioritization structures, and recommendations for a specific adaptation. Nevertheless, they do not feel competent writing a training plan for athletes.
Then, we let LLMs write an exercise program for their hypothetical athlete, and they reflect on the output. Can your favorite large language model plan your training for *pick your goal and athlete*? The simple answer to that is yes! For many people and common goals, AI is doing a great job writing a training structure. It is doing a better job than 80% of human coaches. Sure, there are concerns with this approach. The LLMs do not really “know” training science. They predict words based on their training data, which can sometimes lead to serious hallucinations. They have problems with numbers, and simple repetition recommendations are occasionally off. Otherwise, LLMs are doing great.
But this shouldn’t even surprise us! There is so much information on the internet about how to run a marathon or train for a bigger bench press. Even before AI and social media, writing a basic exercise program was relatively easy- especially for beginners or some specific populations. You could open any book on training and find a good structure. So don’t be surprised if AI is as good as your next best coach!
Interestingly, the students can easily fact-check the AI. They can immediately see the major issues with the outlined training structure and correct them. But it is also a mindboggling experience. They want to learn something that the current LLMs already can do well1.


From a coaching perspective, I differentiate the training process into five phases. Each phase has its purpose and order. Naturally, this is just a rough guideline. The order is not fixed, and an experienced coach often jumps between phases. Writing the exercise program is just a tiny part of the process, which I call “drawing the map”. If you have a map, you are ahead of the game - But only if you have a clear destination, know what you are capable of, and can navigate and adjust if the road is blocked.



Coaching steps during the training process


Therefore, the more interesting question is, can AI do all that? The only way training can work out long-term is when regular adjustments happen according to the physiological and psychological needs of the person in relation to the goals and environmental constraints given. I am definitely not saying that AI can not do that. But this is the Training Science Turing Test we should think about. It is not interesting if it can write down 10 exercises in the correct order and the right repetition zones according to all the books published about training.

title: Exercise Program by AI
text:
Footnotes


The second learning is that they also already understand what good programs look like. That is a surprise to them.↩︎

title: The Non-functionality of Exercise
text:
Terminology of training
In the early 2000s, the term Functional Training was in widespread use. The pioneers who brought functional training to the broader community (mostly known are probably: Gray Cook, Mike Boyle, Greg Glassman, and Mark Verstegen) built a conceptual framework for movement practices to serve human movement effectiveness and efficiency. While their approaches differed from each other, their overarching idea was to build a training concept with broad transfer by implementing movements based on tasks or patterns that recur frequently in sports or daily life. Many of their ideas are built around neurology, motor control, and development from the 1960s onward. Most importantly, they broke with the convention at the time to dissect the human body into single muscles or organ systems. But early on, the Functional Training proponents faced criticism Siff (2002). Especially the name angered many coaches and scientists alike. The argument is that the term Functional implies that other forms of training are non-functional. This criticism of “Functional” Training is valid, but I think that the term is as good as many other descriptions of exercise programs we use. Oftentimes we name a training methodology after its intended outcome. The WHY of Functional training is to gain specific functions. A more precise way of talking about a training method is to talk about the WHAT. To give another example, strength training would be the WHY, while calling it resistance training defines WHAT you do. Making this distinction would open the door for a shift of our attention around movement.



Outcome Goals - the WHY
The language we use when talking about exercise and training is important. It drives our attention and intention around what we do. We use WHY terms to invoke motivation in athletes and the general population. From a public health perspective, talking about strength training could make a lot of sense as the quality and quantity of the skeletal muscle system is a highly relevant metric when it comes to mortality and quality of life in elderly and patients. The whole language around exercise and training is built around the notion of goals. They represent the WHY. Here we come back to the term “functional training”. The dichotomy between functional and non-functional training implies that we know every WHY (i.e., function) that the training has. As I describe in another post, this is even part of the definition of training. The goal lets us focus our attention. However, focus also means that we lose the ability to explore.


Movement practice
By setting goals and calling out the desired outcomes of a training program, we define the meaning of the movement based on future outcomes. An alternative is to let the meaning emerge. The movement only defines the search space, and the interaction with the person creates individual meaning. Instead of a narrow, hard focus, we stay curious, open, and exploratory.
Movement does not have to be functional to be meaningful! It can not serve a specific goal in itself. The goal can only be revealed by the person and in a certain situation. The outcomes it has on our lives can not be predicted and shouldn’t be. This is for sure not an alternative to “Training” - it is an expansion.
I want to finish this off with a saying Peter Drucker is frequently credited for (see this post by Danny Buerkli):
“What gets measured gets managed.”
I think in a sense it is still true, but in reverse: You will often try to manage what you measure. But this also means that you will not even recognize the hidden potential in things you do not measure. A movement practice is a way of opening up to the potential of change we can not foresee. It’s a way to embrace meaning by not installing a predefined purpose.





References

Dominski, Fábio Hech, Ramires Alsamir Tibana, and Alexandro Andrade. 2022. “‘Functional Fitness Training,’ CrossFit, HIMT, or HIFT: What Is the Preferable Terminology?” Frontiers in Sports and Active Living 4.


Ide, Bernardo N., Amanda P. Silvatti, Moacir Marocolo, Clarcson P. C. Santos, Bruno V. C. Silva, Dustin J. Oranchuk, and Gustavo R. Mota. 2021. “Is There Any Non-functional Training? A Conceptual Review.” Frontiers in Sports and Active Living 3 (January): 803366. https://doi.org/10.3389/fspor.2021.803366.


Siff, Mel C. 2002. “Functional Training Revisited.” Strength & Conditioning Journal 24 (5): 42.
